japanese: こんにちは
note: The most common greeting, used from late morning to evening.

japanese: おはようございます
note: Polite morning greeting, used until around 10am.

japanese: こんばんは
note: Used from late afternoon into the night.

japanese: ありがとうございます
note: Polite thank you. Use this one — it's never wrong.

japanese: すみません
note: Use to get attention, apologize lightly, or say 'excuse me'.

japanese: おねがいします
note: Add after a request to be polite. Very versatile.

japanese: はい
note: Simple and polite yes.

japanese: いいえ
note: Polite no. In casual speech, just shake your head gently.

japanese: わかりました
note: Literally 'I understood'. Shows you've got it.

japanese: わかりません
note: Very useful when someone speaks too fast.

japanese: さようなら
note: Formal goodbye. For casual, try 'Ja ne' or just bow.

japanese: ごめんなさい
note: A sincere apology. For lighter situations use 'sumimasen'.

japanese: すみません、通ります
note: Use in crowds or on packed trains.

japanese: たすけてもらえますか？
note: Polite way to ask for help from a stranger.

japanese: にほんごがはなせません
note: Usually understood — people will try to help anyway.

japanese: ゆっくりはなしてください
note: Very helpful when someone is trying to explain something.

japanese: もういちどおねがいします
note: Politely asks someone to repeat themselves.

japanese: えいごはだいじょうぶですか？
note: Ask before switching to English. Many people know some.

japanese: ひとりです
note: Just say the number of people. Simple and understood.

japanese: ふたりです
note: Hold up two fingers to be extra clear.

english: Do you have an English menu?
japanese: えいごのメニューはありますか？
romaji: Eigo no menyuu wa arimasu ka?
note: Many tourist-area restaurants have one.

japanese: これをおねがいします
note: Point at the menu or food display and say this.

japanese: おすすめはなんですか？
note: Great for trying local specialties.

japanese: にくなしでおねがいします
note: Useful for vegetarians. 'Niku' means meat.

japanese: ぶたにくなしでおねがいします
note: 'Butaniku' is pork specifically.

japanese: おみずをください
note: Water is usually free in Japanese restaurants.

japanese: おかいけいおねがいします
note: You usually pay at the register, not at the table.

japanese: カードではらえますか？
note: Cash is still common in Japan. Always good to ask.

japanese: おいしかったです！
note: A great compliment — staff will really appreciate it.

japanese: これはいくらですか？
note: Point at the item while asking.

japanese: ふくろはありますか？
note: Bags usually cost a few yen in convenience stores.

japanese: ふくろはいりません
note: Eco-friendly and appreciated!

japanese: カードではらえますか？
note: Most convenience stores accept cards and IC cards.

japanese: ICカードはつかえますか？
note: Suica, Pasmo, ICOCA — they work almost everywhere.

japanese: レシートをおねがいします
note: Useful for tax-free purchases or expense tracking.

japanese: みているだけです
note: Politely declines help from shop staff.

japanese: これはどこですか？
note: Show a photo or point at what you're looking for.

japanese: えきはどこですか？
note: 'Eki' means train station.

japanese: なんばんせんですか？
note: Ask at the ticket gate or information counter.

japanese: このでんしゃは___にいきますか？
romaji: Kono densha wa ___ ni ikimasu ka?
note: Fill in the station name. Showing it written helps.

japanese: ___にいきたいです
note: Say the place name first, then 'ni ikitai desu'.

japanese: ___までおねがいします
note: Perfect for taxis. Show the address on your phone.

japanese: どのくらいかかりますか？
note: Works for travel time, waiting time, or walking time.

japanese: とおいですか？
note: Useful when deciding whether to walk or take a taxi.

japanese: ひだりにまがってください
note: 'Hidari' means left.

japanese: みぎにまがってください
note: 'Migi' means right.

japanese: まっすぐいってください
note: 'Massugu' means straight ahead.

japanese: よやくしています
note: Say your name after this, or show the booking confirmation.

japanese: チェックインおねがいします
note: Most hotel staff understand 'check-in' in English too.

japanese: にもつをあずけてもいいですか？
romaji: Nimotsu wo azukete mo ii desu ka?
note: Hotels and stations often store bags, even before check-in.

japanese: チェックアウトはなんじですか？
note: Usually 10am or 11am.

japanese: へやのかぎをおねがいします
romaji: Heya no kagi wo onegai shimasu
note: Many modern hotels use card keys.

japanese: Wi-Fiのパスワードをおしえてください
romaji: Waifai no pasuwaado wo oshiete kudasai
note: Usually printed on a card in the room.

english: There is a problem with my room
japanese: へやにもんだいがあります
note: Then point or show what's wrong.

japanese: たすけて！
note: Shout this in an emergency. People will come.

japanese: いしゃがひつようです
note: Hotels can usually arrange a doctor who speaks English.

japanese: きぶんがわるいです
note: Literally 'I feel bad'. Use at a pharmacy or clinic.

japanese: パスポートをなくしました
note: Go to the nearest police box (koban) for help.

japanese: さいふをなくしました
note: Japan has amazing lost-and-found — check with police.

japanese: けいさつをよんでください
note: Police number is 110 in Japan.

japanese: きゅうきゅうしゃをよんでください
note: Ambulance number is 119 in Japan.

japanese: びょういんはどこですか？
note: Ask at your hotel or a police box for directions.

japanese: かんぱい！
note: The classic Japanese toast. Raise your glass and say it!

japanese: ビールをひとつおねがいします
note: Works at any bar, izakaya, or restaurant.

japanese: カクテルはなにがありますか？
romaji: Kakuteru wa nani ga arimasu ka?
note: Good for cocktail bars. Many have picture menus.

english: Do you have a drinks menu?
japanese: ドリンクメニューはありますか？
note: Most bars and izakaya have a separate drinks menu.

japanese: おなじものをおねがいします
note: Point at someone else's drink or your empty glass.

japanese: もういっぱいおねがいします
note: Hold up your empty glass for extra clarity.

english: What Japanese sake do you recommend?
japanese: おすすめのにほんしゅはなんですか？
romaji: Osusume no nihonshu wa nan desu ka?
note: 'Nihonshu' is Japanese sake. Staff love recommending.

japanese: チャージりょうきんはありますか？
note: Many bars have a small cover (otoshi) that includes a snack.

japanese: あまいものをおねがいします
note: Good for fruity cocktails or sweet sake.

japanese: ノンアルコールのドリンクをおねがいします
romaji: Non-arukooru no dorinku wo onegai shimasu
note: Japan has great non-alcoholic beer and cocktail options.

japanese: おかいけいおねがいします
note: Same phrase as restaurants. Pay at the bar or register.

japanese: にほんしゅはありますか？
note: Say 'nihonshu' — just 'sake' can mean any alcohol in Japanese.

japanese: あつかんをおねがいします
note: Warmed sake, great in winter. A classic way to drink it.

japanese: れいしゅをおねがいします
note: Chilled sake. Best for premium sakes to taste the full flavor.

english: What types of sake do you have?
japanese: どんなにほんしゅがありますか？
note: They may offer junmai, ginjo, daiginjo, and more.

japanese: じゅんまいしゅをおねがいします
note: Pure rice sake, no added alcohol. Full-bodied and rich.

japanese: だいぎんじょうをおねがいします
note: Premium sake — light, fruity, and aromatic. Rice polished to 50%+.

english: Can I try a small tasting?
japanese: ちょっとあじみできますか？
note: Many sake bars let you sample before ordering a full glass.

japanese: からくちのおさけをおねがいします
romaji: Karakuchi no osake wo onegai shimasu
note: 'Karakuchi' means dry. Crisp and clean-tasting.

japanese: あまくちのおさけをおねがいします
romaji: Amakuchi no osake wo onegai shimasu
note: 'Amakuchi' means sweet. Smooth and mellow flavor.

japanese: じざけをおねがいします
note: 'Jizake' is locally brewed sake. Every region has its own.

japanese: スパークリングにほんしゅをおねがいします
note: Fizzy sake — light and refreshing. Great for beginners.

japanese: にほんしゅをいっぱいおねがいします
note: Often served in a small wooden box (masu) or ceramic cup.

english: A sake flight / tasting set, please
japanese: のみくらべセットをおねがいします
note: A set of 3 small cups to compare different sakes. Perfect for exploring.

japanese: いち
に
さん
よん
note: Can also be 'shi', but 'yon' is more common.

japanese: ご
きょう
あした
きのう
あさ
ひる / ごご
note: 'Hiru' is noon/daytime, 'gogo' is afternoon.

japanese: よる
いま
あとで
ここ
note: Point while saying it for clarity.

japanese: そこ / あそこ
note: 'Soko' is nearby there, 'asoko' is far over there.

japanese: あつい
note: For weather or temperature.

japanese: さむい
note: For weather. 'Tsumetai' is for cold things/drinks.

japanese: おおきい
ちいさい
